title: Why this economist says WA can’t build its way out of housing crisis
text:
Advertisement

PoliticsWAAustralian economy Why this economist says WA can’t build its way out of housing crisis Hamish Hastie Updated February 28, 2026 — 10:51am ,first published 10:16am Save You have reached your maximum number of saved items. Remove items from your saved list to add more. Save this article for later Add articles to your saved list and come back to them anytime. Got it Share A A A

Western Australia will not be able to build itself out of a housing crisis and should reconsider a sovereign wealth fund with which to guard itself from economic shock in resource downturns. These are the messages former Citibank trader and ‘the people’s economist’ Gary Stevenson will relay to a Perth crowd on Monday night as part of the West Australian leg of his Down Under tour on March 2. Former Citibank trader and popular YouTube economist Gary Stevenson. Youtube/GarysEconomics Stevenson’s Youtube channel has amassed more than 200 million views and 1.5 million subscribers since the start of the pandemic for his simplified and sometimes blunt explanations of economic trends, particularly the explosion in global inequality since COVID-19, driven by stimulus packages. WAtoday put questions about WA’s economy, particularly the state’s housing crisis, to Stevenson, who became a multi-millionaire while working for Citibank, ahead of his show on Monday.

Advertisement

Stevenson said WA’s housing affordability issues were not unique to this state and were the result of a global asset price boom. “Every single person in the entire world thinks the global asset price crisis, including the gold price, including the price of stocks, is caused by their local mayor,” he said. “The context is important, because what you have is clearly a global macroeconomic problem, and everybody is trying to fix it by tinkering around with the rules around housing. This is what drives me a little bit mad here. “We could talk about negative gearing, which is obviously not going to help, we could talk about 5 per cent mortgages, which is obviously not going to help. But at the end of the day, somebody has to step back and say, why are the prices of all assets going up?” Stevenson pins that on trillions in global stimulus packages, including packages like jobseeker here in Australia, trickling through the economy until eventually ending up in the accounts of the wealthy who then sought to invest that cash in assets.

Advertisement

He said that unless global asset prices came down somewhere, Perth would not be able to build enough houses to escape the problem. He warned that continued building of homes on the urban fringe would turn Perth into a slum. Uk economist Gary Stevenson. “Every country that has tried to build its way out of housing crises without dealing with the underlying problem of inequality and distribution has built a slum,” he said. “The whole world is filled with these places that have had no restrictions on housing, and they built a massive shanty town outside of their city.

Advertisement

“And you think that can’t happen in a country like Australia. I grew up in Ilford, which is a poor suburb of East London...it was a poor suburb for working-class families, and it is now a slum. It’s as simple as that. “All respect to the people that live there, is a place of desperate poverty, and those family homes are now expanded backwards, expanded upwards, and rented out by the room. Australia is a bit behind on this, but you’re going in the same direction. You will build a super luxury centre surrounded by extremely low-quality slums.” On WA’s resources endowments Stevenson revised the age-old argument that WA should follow the lead of Norway and its sovereign wealth fund, which holds nearly AUD $3 trillion in assets. “That natural resource wealth, it doesn’t last forever and that’s your inheritance,” he said.

Advertisement

“I understand that wages have been good for a lot of people in Perth and in Western Australia because of the mining boom. “Once that’s gone, that’s gone. Are you giving up your inheritance for 10, 15, 20 years of good living, and do you have a plan for what happens afterwards? “You would not be the first country in the world to squander a great natural resource fortune and not have it benefit the people in the long run.” Stevenson will be on stage at the Regal Theatre in Subiaco on Monday night.

title: Group kicked out of Mardi Gras
text:
A left-wing activist group has been expelled from this year’s Mardi Gras parade after refusing to remove social media posts alleging another group was “pro-genocide”.

The move comes as tens of thousands are expected to descend on Sydney’s CBD on Saturday night for the 48th annual Gay and Lesbian Mardi Gras parade.

Pride in Protest, a group which has previously marched in the parade and describes itself a collective that campaigns for social justice in the queer community, have claimed they were barred from marching by organisers late on Friday.

Advertisement Advertisement

Advertisement Advertisement

In a statement posted to social media, Pride in Protest claims they were told they would be banned unless they deleted posts about Dayenu, another group set to march, for alleged “pro-genocide” views.

Pride in Protest (not pictured) say they have been excluded from the Mardi Gras parade. Picture: NewsWire / Dylan Coker

Dayenu, which describes itself as Sydney's first Jewish LGBTQ+ community social group, had previously pulled out of the Mardi Gras parade over concerns for the safety of it’s members.

The group later switched course and said they would march in the parade for the 26th time.

According to Pride in Protest, Mardi Gras chief executive Jesse Matheson wrote to the group on Friday, saying they would be banned from marching “unless we delete posts calling out Zionist float Dayenu for their ‘pro-genocide’ stance”.

Advertisement Advertisement

Advertisement Advertisement

Pride in Protest have accused Mardi Gras organisers of censorship and have called for a protest at Sydney’s Town Hall on Saturday afternoon.

In comments published by the Sydney Morning Herald, Dayenu said it was “deeply offended” by Pride in Protest’s allegations.

“Dayenu is not a Zionist organisation, and we feel that word is being used to insult our Jewish community, in place of the word Jew or Jewish,” they said.

More to come.

title: Nostalgic five-word advert slogan Aussies didn't know was pinched for New Zealand
text:
Since the 1980s, the advertising jingle “Aussie kids are Weet-Bix kids” made generations of youngsters feel a special connection to the popular breakfast food. But little did they know that just across the Tasman Sea, the same tune was being played with the words “Kiwi kids are Weet-Bix kids”.

Professor Gary Mortimer, an expert in consumer behaviour and retail marketing at Queensland University of Technology, explained that many “older consumers” feel nostalgia for advertisements of their childhood.

“In a time when most marketing focuses heavily on the future – buy this product now and later, you’ll be happier, healthier, or more productive – nostalgic marketing transports us back to a simpler, happier, safer period of our lives,” he told Yahoo News.

Advertisement Advertisement

Advertisement Advertisement

The Weet-Bix jingle particularly resonated with Gen X or Millennials, and over the decades, they became habituated to hearing the same words when the advertisement played.

Twenty years ago, few people knew of these localised changes to jingles, but with the rise in popularity of social media, awareness has grown as users share their local television commercials and receive international attention.

So hearing an important word like “Aussie” changed to “Kiwi” creates a "sensitivity".

Professor Mortimer added that changing the words to a jingle is a common commercial decision.

“They don’t want to reinvent the wheel and create a new slogan, they just want to change a particular element of it, and mostly that’s about localisation,” he said.

In this 1989 commercial, Victorian viewers were still told that Safeway were the "Fresh Food People". Source: Woolworths

Who are the Fresh Food People?

Another example is a single word difference to the popular “Fresh Food People” jingle, depending on where you watched television in Australia.

Expert praises Vegemite advertising after moving to Australia

RMIT marketing and design expert Dr Argho Bandyopadhyay grew up in India, and so he developed a nostalgia for different brands before migrating to Australia in 2011.

One of his strongest recollections is television advertising for Nirma, a brand of washing powder.

A still from the 1992 Kiwi Kids are Weet-Bix Kids commercial. Source: Sanitarium

Not only does he remember hearing the jingle and seeing its distinctive blue colour, but he also has fond associations of shopping for it with his mother, and her using it to clean his clothes.

In Australia, he was “surprised” to learn that discussions about Vegemite rekindled the same responses in his peers.

“They had the Happy Little Vegemites jingle that focused on kids, and everyone knew what it was,” he said.

“Vegemite has done a very good job, they remember it not only because of the taste, but also the advertising and the iconic [red and yellow] colours.”

Dr Argho Bandyopadhyay feels nostalgia for the Nirma washing powder commercials he saw as a child. Source: Nirma

New generations may not share same advertising nostalgia

As traditional television viewership declines and mass advertising is replaced with targeted content for individuals, future generations may no longer share the same collective memories of commercials.

Dr Bandyopadhyay remembers sitting down to dinner at 5.30pm and seeing Nirma advertised on the television.

But today, he notes the connection between consumers and products is now built through content from influencers and through dialogues with companies on their social media pages.

He believes the emotional connection we once shared with brands now occurs slowly as we receive messages without the same coordination across different platforms.

“That emotional connection actually dilutes,” he said.
